era_context: The reign of Roman Emperor Marcus Aurelius (161–180 CE), a period of Stoic philosophy and military consolidation. The Antonine Plague (165–180 CE) continues to ravage the empire. In China, the Eastern Han Dynasty is in decline, with eunuch factions and regional warlords fragmenting central authority. The Parthian Empire remains Rome's primary eastern rival.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (sparse archaeological evidence for this specific year)

events.title: Marcus Aurelius campaigns on the Danube frontier
events.region: Roman Empire, Danube frontier (modern-day Hungary, Serbia, Romania)
events.description: Marcus Aurelius continues his Marcomannic Wars against Germanic and Sarmatian tribes along the Danube. This year marks sustained military operations to contain incursions and stabilize the frontier. The campaign is documented in the Historia Augusta and contemporary sources, though specific battles are often conflated across years. The emperor's presence on the frontier reinforces Roman military discipline and strategy.

sources.name: Historia Augusta, Life of Marcus Aurelius

events.certainty_note: The Marcomannic Wars span 166–180 CE; specific events within individual years are often poorly dated. This entry reflects the general campaign activity attested for the period, not a discrete event datable to 170 specifically.

events.title: Antonine Plague continues in Roman Empire
events.description: The Antonine Plague (likely smallpox or measles, introduced via Parthian trade routes c. 165 CE) continues its devastating sweep through the empire. Mortality is particularly severe in urban centers and military camps. Contemporary sources report mass deaths, economic disruption, and labor shortages. The plague contributes to military recruitment difficulties and fiscal strain.

sources.name: Galen, On the Affected Parts and other medical writings

events.certainty_note: The plague is well-attested in contemporary medical and historical sources. Galen's descriptions are firsthand. Dating to specific years within the plague's span (165–180) is approximate, but the plague's continuation in 170 is certain.

events.title: Eastern Han Dynasty fragmentation accelerates
events.description: The Eastern Han court at Luoyang faces mounting instability as eunuch factions (the Ten Eunuchs) consolidate power and regional warlords assert autonomy. Imperial authority weakens; provincial governors increasingly act independently. This period of decline sets the stage for the Yellow Turban Rebellion (184 CE) and the eventual collapse of the dynasty (220 CE).

sources.name: Records of the Three Kingdoms (Sanguozhi), compiled by Chen Shou (3rd century CE)
Book of the Later Han (Hou Han Shu), compiled by Fan Ye (5th century CE)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Han decline is well-documented, but specific events within individual years are often poorly dated in surviving sources. The year 170 falls within the documented period of eunuch dominance and regional fragmentation.

disconfirming_evidence: The specific dating of individual Marcomannic War engagements to 170 CE is uncertain; modern scholarship treats the entire campaign (166–180) as a continuous conflict with poorly differentiated annual events. The Antonine Plague's identification as smallpox or measles remains debated; some scholars propose other pathogens. The 'Ten Eunuchs' as a discrete faction is a historiographic construct; eunuch influence in the Han court was more diffuse and fluctuating than this label suggests.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 170 CE is moderate but unevenly distributed. Roman sources (Cassius Dio, Historia Augusta) are relatively abundant but written decades or centuries after events, introducing narrative bias and conflation. Chinese sources are similarly retrospective compilations. The Antonine Plague is well-attested in medical and historical sources, but specific mortality figures are unreliable. The year 170 itself is not a natural boundary in any of these narratives; it is an arbitrary point in longer processes of military, epidemiological, and political change. Western-centric bias is present in the surviving record: Roman and Chinese sources dominate; Parthian, Indian, and African perspectives are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: Marcomannic Wars are part of the broader Roman military consolidation following the Parthian War
Antonine Plague introduced via Parthian trade routes during the Parthian War
Han fragmentation and eunuch dominance create conditions for Yellow Turban Rebellion
